dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 3 responding for difficulty breathing.
Hi, I'm Alex, an EMT with the rescue squad. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having difficulty breathing-started about 30 minutes ago.
Can you tell me your name and where we are?
My name is Sarah, and we are in [CITY].
What is today's date, please?
September 26, 2019.
We need to do a quick airway check. Can you take a deep breath?
Your airway is clear and maintainable with no signs of obstruction.
Are you using any extra effort to breathe or sound more labored?
Yes, I see that you're using your accessory muscles. Your breathing is labored and rapid.
Pulse is 119-strong and regular. Skin is warm, cap refill is less than 2 seconds.
Ma'am, we're going to check your breathing. Let me listen to your chest.
Can you tell me about your medical history?
I have a history of three strokes and hypertension. I take amlodipine and aspirin.
Excellent-Sarah, you are alert and oriented to all four levels.
Do you have allergies I need to be aware of?
No known drug allergies.
Let's take a few vitals. Pulse is 119.
Respirations are at 33 per minute-rapid and deep.
Blood pressure is 159 over 95.
Oxygen saturation is 97%.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia with no signs of cardiac compromise.
Next, we'll obtain a 12-lead EKG for more detailed cardiac monitoring.
Patency of airway is good. No sounds of obstruction, no stridor.
I can see you are using upper chest accessory muscles to breathe due to the work of breathing.
We are putting on a 4-lead ECG now as part of the process.
Administering albuterol and Atrovent via nebulizer to help with the bronchospastic condition.
Oxygen is being delivered via nebulizer to increase oxygen levels.
We will place an IV in your arm or leg for medication administration soon.
Following protocol 3-11 for respiratory distress and acute bronchospasm.
Respirations have improved slightly to 22 per minute.
Blood pressure is now 142 over 90. Pulse remains at 104.
Oxygen saturation is now 99%.
Administering 10mg dexamethasone intravenously to control inflammation in the airways.
Listen to your chest again-wheezes are decreasing, but still some present.
The 12-lead EKG showed signs of left ventricular hypertrophy, likely from your blood pressure history.
We are now ready to move you to the stretcher for transport in a secure position.
What hospital would you prefer to be taken to?
Please take me to [COMPANY_NAME] for evaluation.
Understood, we're heading to [COMPANY_NAME] with ambulance lights and sirens.
Sarah, can I access your arm for an IV? You were lying down and unbothered earlier.
Yes, your arm is fine.
You're all set in the stretcher. The drive won't be long, and we're making sure you're safe.
Another round of albuterol and Atrovent nebulization is being given now.
Rechecking your breathing-SpO2 looks stable and breathing improved slightly from last time.
We are en route to the hospital as soon as you're loaded.
EKG shows persistent sinus tachycardia but no acute cardiac changes at this point.
The 12-lead EKG has been transmitted and is under review.
Glucose check is at 113 mg/dL-within normal limits.
The ER nurse has been notified and will meet us in trauma bay seven.
Ambulance is ready for departure and maintaining lights and sirens.
Glucose is still at 113-no changes reported.
Pulse is at 104 as of now.
4-lead ECG remains stable with no acute cardiac issue trends.
Sarah seems significantly better than when we arrived-breathing effort is much lower.
There is ongoing improvement with only minimal wheezing now audible.
Looking stable. No signs of increased respiratory distress or new cardiac findings.
Oxygen is running and her breathing is even, much better than initial readings.
No pain reported. Pain score is 0.
Transfer to the trauma team at [COMPANY_NAME] is ongoing now.
Respiratory rate is down further to 16 per minute since we started our interventions.
SPO2 is 98% and stable after the last nebulizer treatment.
Blood pressure is 128 over 82 with pulse at 103 now.
Transfer of care is complete with the trauma nurse at the facility.
You mentioned something like a cold for a couple of days. When did the symptoms start?
I feel like I caught something about two days ago; it was minor at first.
When was the last time you ate or drank anything?
I had a glass of water at around 10 this morning.
Another nebulizer with a combination of albuterol and Atrovent is about to be administered.
Rechecking your breathing-responsive with still some mild wheezing, but not concerning now.
Pulse is at 103 now-staying in a good range.
SpO2 is at 98% still with no drop and no signs of compromise.
You're in good shape. We're preparing to move you onto the hospital stretcher now.
GCS is 15: full eye opening, normal verbal, motor response.
The final nebulizer treatment with albuterol and Atrovent is given for the transport trip ahead.
All systems are set for hospital transfer with no risks predicted.
IV in the left arm is going smoothly, fluids are stable.
Breathing looks closer to baseline with less added effort now.
Glucose stayed at 113-no fluctuations causing any concern.
You also had three cerebral vascular events-strokes-can you tell us when these occurred?
Yes, my last stroke was a couple of years ago, but I've managed since with my medications.
We'll continue ECG monitoring for any cardiac changes during transport.
Vital signs remain stable and within expected boundaries.
Dexamethasone IV was given and completed in full at 23:10.
Breathing is improved with a mild bit of tachypnea still present.
You're being transported in a flat position with all monitoring connections secure.
Lights and sirens are on to allow faster and safer transport to the ER.
EKG findings are still consistent with our baseline readings.
Blood pressure is the same at 128 over 82 during transport.
Adjusting your stretcher position for full access to IV and any emergency procedures.
All necessary documentation and checklists are complete for the hospital handoff.
We have good, smooth transportation with minor traffic considerations.
We're entering the hospital now, Sarah. You're doing really well.
Handoff into the emergency services with no complications expected or seen.
All IV lines are secured and properly labeled for hospital nurses to take over.
She is responding strongly and following directions, which I think is very encouraging.
She is recovering well-faster than I expected based on her early condition.
All pre-transport care(checks and care) have been concluded without incident.
All actions were performed in line with EMS protocol and documented throughout.
